articles.topic: inflammation as root cause of heart disease
articles.headline: Cholesterol Isn't the Only Villain. Inflammation May Be the Real Driver of Heart Disease.
articles.body: For years, cholesterol got most of the blame for heart attacks. But a growing body of research is pointing to something else: chronic inflammation. The May 2026 cover story in Scientific American spotlights the scientists who've spent decades untangling why up to a quarter of heart attack patients have none of the classic risk factors. The answer, they believe, is low-grade, persistent inflammation silently damaging artery walls. The good news: an old, cheap anti-inflammatory drug called colchicine is showing real promise. Used for generations to treat gout, it's now been shown in major trials to reduce cardiovascular events in people with coronary artery disease.
articles.takeaway: Ask your doctor about your inflammation markers, not just your cholesterol. CRP levels may be a better predictor of your future heart risk than LDL alone.

articles.topic: cognitive improvement possible at any age
articles.headline: Science Just Confirmed: Your Brain Can Keep Getting Sharper at Any Age
articles.body: Good news if you're worried about cognitive decline: a landmark three-year study from the University of Texas at Dallas tracked nearly 4,000 adults aged 19 to 94 and found that brain performance can measurably improve at any age. Using a multidimensional measure called the BrainHealth Index, researchers tracked thinking skills, social connection, and emotional resilience. People who consistently engaged with brain-healthy habits and strategy-based training showed the biggest gains. And here's the part that surprises most people: younger adults made gains equal to those in their 70s and 80s. Starting early isn't just for seniors.
articles.takeaway: Targeted, consistent mental engagement matters more than age. Strategy-based thinking, social connection, and brain-healthy habits all drive measurable improvement in cognitive function.

articles.topic: fat cell protein function obesity metabolism
articles.headline: Scientists Just Found a Hidden Role for a Protein Inside Your Fat Cells
articles.body: A fat-burning enzyme called HSL has been understood since the 1960s as the body's fuel switch, releasing stored fat when energy drops. New research from the University of Toulouse just upended that picture. Scientists discovered HSL is also operating inside the nucleus of fat cells, where it helps regulate how healthy those cells stay. Lose that function, and fat tissue starts breaking down in dangerous ways. The finding reframes how we think about body fat: it's not just how much you carry, but whether your fat tissue is functioning properly. The quality of fat cells may matter as much as quantity for long-term metabolic health.
articles.takeaway: Fat cell health is about more than size. Lifestyle habits that support metabolic function, including regular movement and adequate protein, help fat tissue work properly, not just shrink.

articles.headline: The Biggest Strength Training Update in 17 Years Just Dropped. The Message Is Simple.
articles.body: The American College of Sports Medicine just released its first major resistance training Position Stand since 2009, analyzing 137 systematic reviews covering more than 30,000 participants. The headline? You don't need a complicated plan. The biggest health jump happens when someone goes from doing zero resistance training to doing any. Barbells, bands, bodyweight, home routines: the research shows they all work. For strength, lift heavy at least twice a week. For muscle size, pile up the weekly sets. And the old rules about needing constant variety or fancy equipment? The new guidance officially calls those myths.
articles.takeaway: Train your major muscle groups at least twice a week. The specific equipment matters far less than showing up consistently. Any resistance training is better than none.
articles.source: Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise / ACSM

quote.text: The secret of getting ahead is getting started.
quote.author: Mark Twain
quote.context: American author and humorist, widely regarded as one of the greatest writers in US history.

workout.title: 90-Second Power Surges: Speed Play for Busy Days
workout.tip: If you're running with a stroller or on a tight schedule, shorten the surge to 60 seconds and extend the easy jog. The work-to-rest ratio matters more than the exact duration.
workout.warmup: Start with 5 minutes of easy walking or very light jogging at a pace where you can hold a full conversation. Then do 4-6 leg swings on each side and 30 seconds of high knees to wake up your hips and calves before the first surge.

exercises.name: Easy Jog Warm-Up Block
exercises.notes: Conversational pace only. You should be able to say a full sentence without gasping.

exercises.name: Hard Surge
exercises.notes: Push to a strong, controlled effort — about a 7 or 8 out of 10. Not an all-out sprint, but clearly harder than your easy pace. Think: comfortably uncomfortable.

exercises.name: Easy Recovery Jog
exercises.notes: Drop back to easy, conversational pace between each surge. Let your breathing settle before the next hard effort.

exercises.name: Easy Cool-Down Jog
exercises.notes: Slow right down. Keep the legs moving at an easy shuffle to flush out the effort from the surges.

workout.cooldown: Walk for 2-3 minutes and do a gentle standing quad stretch and calf stretch on each leg. Hold each for 20-30 seconds to reduce post-run tightness.

product.name: HOKA Clifton 9
product.blurb: Max cushion for easy days and long runs. Keeps your legs fresh for hard sessions.

meal.title: Lemon Herb Egg and White Bean Power Skillet
ingredients: 1 cup canned white beans, drained and rinsed
Zest and juice of half a lemon
Salt and black pepper to taste
Fresh parsley or dill to finish (optional)
meal.instructions: Heat olive oil in a medium skillet over medium heat. Add garlic and red pepper flakes, cook 60 seconds until fragrant. Add white beans and cook 2-3 minutes until lightly golden. Stir in spinach until wilted, then squeeze lemon juice over everything. Create three small wells in the mixture and crack an egg into each. Cover and cook 3-4 minutes until whites are set but yolks are still slightly runny. Finish with lemon zest, feta, and fresh herbs.
meal.tip: Make this even faster on weekday mornings by draining and rinsing the beans the night before and storing them in a small container in the fridge. The whole skillet comes together in under 12 minutes.
